packs.name: Terraform
packs.description: Protects against destructive Terraform operations like destroy, taint, and apply with -auto-approve

structural_patterns.name: destroy
structural_patterns.reason: terraform destroy removes ALL managed infrastructure. Use 'terraform plan -destroy' first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform destroy removes ALL managed infrastructure:

- Every resource in your state file is destroyed
- Cloud resources (VMs, databases, networks) deleted
- Cannot be undone without backups/recreation
- Use -target to destroy specific resources only

Preview first: terraform plan -destroy

structural_patterns.name: apply-auto-approve
structural_patterns.reason: terraform apply -auto-approve skips confirmation. Remove -auto-approve for safety.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform apply -auto-approve skips confirmation:

- No opportunity to review changes before applying
- Intended for CI/CD, not interactive use
- Changes may destroy or recreate resources

For safety: remove -auto-approve and review the plan

structural_patterns.name: taint
structural_patterns.reason: terraform taint marks a resource to be destroyed and recreated on next apply.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform taint marks resource for recreation:

- Resource will be destroyed on next apply
- New resource created with same config
- May cause downtime during recreation
- IP addresses and identifiers may change

Use -replace in plan/apply instead (Terraform 0.15.2+)

structural_patterns.name: state-rm
structural_patterns.reason: terraform state rm removes resource from state without destroying it. Resource becomes unmanaged.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform state rm orphans resources:

- Resource removed from Terraform state
- Actual cloud resource still exists
- Resource becomes 'unmanaged' (Terraform ignores it)
- May cause drift between state and reality

Back up state first: terraform state pull > backup.tfstate

structural_patterns.name: state-mv
structural_patterns.reason: terraform state mv moves resources in state. Incorrect moves can cause resource recreation.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform state mv moves resources in state:

- Renames resource address in state file
- Wrong move can cause destruction/recreation
- Use -dry-run to preview the move first
- Does not affect actual cloud resources

Preview first: terraform state mv -dry-run SOURCE DEST

structural_patterns.name: force-unlock
structural_patterns.reason: terraform force-unlock removes state lock. Only use if lock is stale.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform force-unlock removes state locks:

- Forces removal of a state lock
- May cause corruption if another process is running
- Only use when you're sure no other operation is active
- Lock ID required to prevent accidents

Verify no other operations: check CI/CD pipelines, other users

structural_patterns.name: workspace-delete
structural_patterns.reason: terraform workspace delete removes a workspace. Ensure it's not in use.
structural_patterns.explanation:
terraform workspace delete removes workspace:

- Workspace and its state file deleted
- Does NOT destroy actual infrastructure
- Resources become unmanaged (orphaned)
- Cannot be undone without state backup

Destroy resources first: terraform destroy, then delete workspace

packs.name: Ansible
packs.description: Protects against destructive Ansible operations like dangerous shell commands and unchecked playbook runs

structural_patterns.name: shell-rm-rf
structural_patterns.reason: Ansible shell/command with 'rm -rf' is destructive. Review carefully.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Running 'rm -rf' via Ansible shell or command module executes destructive deletion across all targeted hosts simultaneously. This multiplies the impact compared to running it locally:

- Files deleted on every host in inventory or pattern
- No confirmation or dry-run by default
- Parallel execution means rapid, widespread destruction
- Cannot be undone without backups on each host

Safer alternatives:
- Use file module with state=absent for managed deletion
- Add --check flag to preview which hosts would be affected
- Use --limit to restrict to specific hosts first
- Verify target path with debug module before deletion

structural_patterns.name: shell-reboot
structural_patterns.reason: Ansible shell/command with reboot/shutdown affects system availability.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Rebooting or shutting down systems via Ansible affects all targeted hosts at once. This can cause service outages across your infrastructure:

- All services on affected hosts become unavailable
- No graceful draining of connections or traffic
- Database transactions may be interrupted
- Load balancer health checks will fail

Safer alternatives:
- Use the reboot module which handles wait_for_connection
- Add serial: 1 to playbook for rolling reboots
- Use --limit to test on single host first
- Coordinate with maintenance windows

structural_patterns.name: extra-vars-delete
structural_patterns.reason: Ansible extra-vars contains potentially destructive keywords. Review carefully.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Extra variables containing destructive keywords like 'delete', 'remove', 'destroy', or 'drop' may trigger conditional destructive actions in playbooks:

- Variables may control whether resources are deleted
- Typos in variable values could trigger unintended paths
- Variable precedence may override safer defaults

Safer alternatives:
- Review playbook to understand how variables are used
- Add --check to see what tasks would run
- Use --limit to test on single host first
- Consider using vault-encrypted vars for destructive flags

packs.name: Pulumi
packs.description: Protects against destructive Pulumi operations like destroy and up with -y (auto-approve)

structural_patterns.name: destroy
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi destroy removes ALL managed infrastructure. Use 'pulumi preview --diff' first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi destroy removes ALL managed infrastructure:

- Every resource in your stack is destroyed
- Cloud resources (VMs, databases, networks) deleted
- Cannot be undone without backups/recreation
- Use --target to destroy specific resources only

Preview first: pulumi preview --diff

structural_patterns.name: up-yes
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi up -y skips confirmation. Remove -y flag for safety.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi up -y skips confirmation:

- No opportunity to review changes before applying
- Intended for CI/CD, not interactive use
- Changes may destroy or recreate resources
- Replacements can cause downtime

For safety: remove -y and review the preview

structural_patterns.name: state-delete
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi state delete removes resource from state without destroying it.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi state delete orphans resources:

- Resource removed from Pulumi state
- Actual cloud resource still exists
- Resource becomes 'unmanaged' (Pulumi ignores it)
- May cause drift between state and reality

Consider: pulumi refresh to sync state with reality

structural_patterns.name: stack-rm
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi stack rm removes the stack. Use --force only if stack is empty.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi stack rm removes the entire stack:

- Stack and its state deleted
- Does NOT destroy actual infrastructure (unless empty)
- --force required if resources still exist
- Resources become unmanaged (orphaned)

Destroy resources first: pulumi destroy, then rm stack

structural_patterns.name: refresh-yes
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi refresh -y auto-approves state changes. Review changes first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi refresh -y auto-approves state sync:

- Syncs Pulumi state with actual cloud resources
- May delete resources from state if not found
- May update state with drift from cloud

Run without -y first to review detected changes

structural_patterns.name: cancel
structural_patterns.reason: pulumi cancel terminates an in-progress update, which may leave resources in inconsistent state.
structural_patterns.explanation:
pulumi cancel stops in-progress operations:

- Terminates currently running update/destroy
- Resources may be left in inconsistent state
- Some resources created, others not
- May require manual cleanup

Use only when operation is stuck/hung

packs.name: AWS CDK
packs.description: Protects against destructive AWS CDK operations like cdk destroy

structural_patterns.name: cdk-destroy
structural_patterns.reason: cdk destroy permanently removes the specified stack and its resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Destroying a CDK stack removes all resources defined in that stack from your AWS account. This can include databases, compute instances, and networking infrastructure. Data in resources without a retention policy will be permanently lost.

Preview changes first:
  cdk diff

List stacks:
  cdk ls

packs.name: Serverless Framework
packs.description: Protects against destructive Serverless operations like sls remove

structural_patterns.name: sls-remove
structural_patterns.reason: sls remove permanently deletes the deployed service and its resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a Serverless service deletes the CloudFormation stack and all associated resources (Lambda, API Gateway, etc.) in your provider. This is irreversible and will stop all service functionality immediately.

Check service info first:
  sls info
